skill.name: statsmodels
skill.description: Guides rigorous statistical modeling with Python statsmodels, including regression, GLM, discrete choice, time series, diagnostics, and inference workflows.
skill.summary: Apply statsmodels for regression, GLM, time series, diagnostics, and publication-ready inference.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: AI review found the static analyzer alerts are false positives from Markdown documentation and statistical Python examples. No executable skill code, prompt injection, credential access, unauthorized network request, or malicious intent was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static False Positive: Markdown Code Blocks
low_findings.description: The many Ruby/shell backtick findings are Markdown fences and inline code examples in documentation. I found no evidence that these strings execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown code fences or statistical Python examples. They are not executable skill hooks, and no command runner is defined in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Static False Positive: Statistical Terms Matched Security Keywords
low_findings.description: High-severity C2 and weak cryptography alerts map to statistical labels such as HC2, HC3, ACF, PACF, descriptive statistics, and Markdown prose. I found no cryptographic implementation or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are standard statsmodels and statistics terminology. The surrounding context is model diagnostics, plots, and descriptive statistics, not encryption or network control code.

low_findings.title: Static False Positive: Python Imports and Plot Labels
low_findings.description: Dynamic import and function-constructor alerts correspond to normal Python import statements and matplotlib title-setting examples. I found no JavaScript import expression, eval path, or dynamic function construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Python documentation examples, not JavaScript runtime constructs. There is no user-controlled code evaluation or plugin script entry point.

low_findings.title: Static False Positive: Official Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings are links to official statsmodels documentation and examples. I found no evidence of outbound requests, tracking pixels, webhooks, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only as reference links for users. The skill contains Markdown guidance and no code path that makes network calls.

low_findings.title: Benign Documentation Search Commands
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts are documentation examples that show users how to search local reference files with grep. They do not gather system information or transmit results.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The grep examples are local documentation search instructions. They are visible to the user and do not include host enumeration, secret access, or network transfer.

content.value_statement: Statistical modeling often requires correct model choice, diagnostics, and careful inference. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through statsmodels workflows for regression, GLM, time series, and econometrics.
actual_capabilities: Guides OLS, WLS, GLS, quantile regression, and mixed effects model selection.
Explains GLM workflows for logistic, Poisson, Gamma, and related outcome types.
Provides time series guidance for ARIMA, SARIMAX, VAR, forecasting, and stationarity checks.
Covers discrete choice workflows for binary, multinomial, ordinal, count, and zero-inflated models.
Recommends diagnostics for heteroskedasticity, autocorrelation, normality, outliers, and influential observations.
Supports interpretation of coefficient tables, robust standard errors, confidence intervals, and model comparisons.
limitations: Requires users to supply clean data and define the research question.
Does not execute models without a Python environment and required libraries.
Cannot guarantee valid inference when assumptions or study design are flawed.
Does not replace expert review for regulated, clinical, or financial decisions.

use_cases.title: Build Regression Models
use_cases.description: Fit and interpret linear models with robust standard errors, diagnostic tests, and coefficient summaries.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Econometric Evidence
use_cases.description: Compare model specifications, test assumptions, and document inference choices for applied research.

use_cases.title: Forecast Time Series
use_cases.description: Choose ARIMA or SARIMAX models, check stationarity, inspect residuals, and report forecast intervals.

prompt_templates.title: Fit a Basic OLS Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use statsmodels to fit an OLS regression for my outcome and predictors. Add an intercept, summarize coefficients, and list the first diagnostics I should check.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Model Family
prompt_templates.prompt: My outcome is [describe outcome]. Recommend the appropriate statsmodels model family, explain why, and identify assumptions and diagnostics.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Model Problems
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these statsmodels results for heteroskedasticity, autocorrelation, normality, multicollinearity, and influential observations. Recommend corrections.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Complex Specifications
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these competing statsmodels specifications using AIC, BIC, likelihood ratio tests where valid, robust standard errors, and practical interpretability.

output_examples.input: Fit an OLS model and explain the diagnostic results.
output_examples.output: The model summary should report coefficient estimates, robust standard errors, p-values, confidence intervals, and R-squared. The diagnostic review should state whether residual patterns, heteroskedasticity, or influential observations threaten inference.

output_examples.input: Help choose between Poisson and Negative Binomial regression.
output_examples.output: Start with the count outcome and expected mean-variance relationship. Use Poisson for equidispersion, test for overdispersion, and prefer Negative Binomial when variance clearly exceeds the mean.

output_examples.input: Plan a SARIMAX forecast for monthly sales.
output_examples.output: The workflow should check stationarity, identify seasonal structure, compare candidate orders, inspect residual diagnostics, and report forecasts with uncertainty intervals.

best_practices: Add an intercept deliberately and document when you omit it.
Check model assumptions before interpreting coefficients or forecasts.
Report confidence intervals and practical effect sizes with p-values.
anti_patterns: Do not use OLS for binary or count outcomes without justification.
Do not choose models only by the smallest p-value.
Do not ignore convergence warnings or residual diagnostic failures.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when you need statsmodels guidance for regression, GLM, discrete choice, time series, diagnostics, or statistical inference.

faq.question: Does it run Python code for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, examples, and review steps. Execution depends on your Python environment and installed packages.

faq.question: Can it choose the correct model automatically?
faq.answer: It can recommend a model based on your outcome, data structure, and goal. You still need to verify assumptions and design limits.

faq.question: What diagnostics does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers heteroskedasticity, autocorrelation, normality, multicollinearity, outliers, influence, stationarity, and convergence checks.

faq.question: Is this only for econometrics?
faq.answer: No. It also supports general data analysis, forecasting, generalized linear models, and applied statistical reporting.

faq.question: How should I provide data context?
faq.answer: Describe the outcome, predictors, sample size, data structure, missing values, and the decision you need to make.
